perturbation.original_text: take all reasonably necessary precautions to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise at least the same degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties would with its own proprietary information
perturbation.changed_text: take precautions as they deem appropriate to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise a degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties see fit with their own proprietary information
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'precautions as they deem appropriate' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, making it subjective and potentially less rigorous. It replaces an objective 'reasonably necessary precautions' with a subjective standard. Similarly, 'a degree of care as they see fit' replaces the requirement of exercising at least the same degree of care. This creates ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language, allowing 'precautions as they deem appropriate' and a degree of care 'as they see fit', could fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection, potentially nullifying trade secret status if the precautions are deemed inadequate by a court. This undermines the intended protection afforded by the NDA under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'reasonably necessary precautions' and 'at least the same degree of care.' The changed text weakens these obligations by allowing 'precautions as they deem appropriate' and a degree of care 'as they see fit.' This introduces a subjective standard, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection as explained in the Law Explanation. Section 5304 of the Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes discusses damages for misappropriation, implying a standard of care is expected. The modified language could lead to a complainant being unable to recover damages if the precautions taken are deemed inadequate, undermining the intended protection. Therefore, the change creates a significant risk of non-compliance with the legal standard of reasonable efforts.

perturbation.original_text: promptly advise the Disclosing Party in writing upon learning of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: endeavor to advise the Disclosing Party upon becoming generally aware of potential unauthorized uses or disclosures of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'promptly advise' with 'endeavor to advise' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The original text requires a specific and immediate action, whereas the modified text suggests an attempt to advise without guaranteeing actual notification. The addition of 'becoming generally aware of potential' also creates uncertainty regarding the threshold for triggering the notification obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under PUTSA, a party who misappropriates a trade secret can be liable for damages. Delay in notification impacts the Disclosing Party's ability to mitigate damage. By weakening the 'promptly advise' obligation, the change hampers the Disclosing Party's ability to take timely action in response to misappropriation, potentially increasing damages and prejudicing their rights under PUTSA. The duty to notify the Disclosing party of potential misappropriation can be grounds for attorney's fees if not met, and can contribute to the grounds for punitive damages.

A court may award reasonable attorney fees, expenses and costs to the prevailing party: (1) if a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith; (2) a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith; or (3) willful and malicious misappropriation exists. perturbation.justification: The original text's requirement to 'promptly advise' upon learning of unauthorized use is weakened by the changed text 'endeavor to advise...upon becoming generally aware of potential unauthorized uses'. The original text creates a clear, time-sensitive obligation, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity and reduces the certainty of notification. While the provided law snippet focuses on attorney's fees related to trade secret misappropriation (12 PA Cons Stat § 5305), the law explanation ties this to PUTSA and the importance of timely notification to mitigate damages from misappropriation. The modification weakens the obligation to notify, which could increase damages and prejudice the disclosing party's rights under trade secret law, making the ambiguous alteration a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ensure that they are under written agreement with the Recipient that establishes non -disclosure and limited use restrictions regarding the Pro prietary Information that are no less restrictive than those set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: use commercially reasonable efforts to have them under an agreement with the Recipient that addresses non-disclosure and use of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a 'written agreement' with specific restrictions. The modified text replaces this with 'commercially reasonable efforts to have them under an agreement that addresses' which weakens the obligation in several ways: It removes the requirement for a *written* agreement, opens up the possibility that a verbal one will suffice (problematic given evidentiary issues), removes the requirement that the agreement be 'no less restrictive', and weakens the requirement from agreements which 'establish non-disclosure and limited use' to agreements which merely 'addresses non-disclosure and use'.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA protects trade secrets if reasonable efforts are made to maintain their secrecy. Requiring a specific written non-disclosure agreement is a reasonable effort. However, 'commercially reasonable efforts to have an agreement that addresses' introduces ambiguity; an informal understanding might not be considered sufficient to maintain secrecy, particularly if misappropriation occurs. This could jeopardize trade secret protection, as the burden is on the trade secret holder to prove adequate measures were in place. The language weakens those measures, thereby lessening the likelihood of succeeding on a claim for misappropriation, particularly when injunctive relief would be necessary under 12 Pa.C.S. § 5302.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the protection of trade secrets, weakening the original obligation to maintain secrecy through a written agreement. The law explanation suggests that 'commercially reasonable efforts' and merely 'addressing' non-disclosure might not be sufficient to maintain secrecy, jeopardizing trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2, which defines 'Misappropriation' in 12 Pa.C.S. § 5302, implies that a weaker agreement could lead to misappropriation if trade secrets are disclosed or used without consent. The original text's requirement of a 'written agreement' ensured a clear and enforceable obligation, while the changed text's 'commercially reasonable efforts to have an agreement that addresses' weakens this protection, creating a risk of non-compliance with trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will not export, re -export, resell or transfer, or otherwise ship or deliver any Proprietary Information, product, assembly, component or any technical data or software which violates any export control s or limitations imposed by the United States or any other governmental authority, or to any country for which an export license or other governmental approval is required at the time of export without first obtaining all necessary licenses or other approv als.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will use reasonable efforts to avoid exporting, re -exporting, reselling or transferring, or otherwise shipping or delivering any Proprietary Information, product, assembly, component or any technical data or software in a manner that knowingly violates any export controls or limitations imposed by the United States or any other governmental authority, or to any country for which an export license or other governmental approval is required.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict prohibition on exporting or transferring proprietary information in violation of export controls. The modified text weakens this prohibition by: (1) replacing 'agrees that it will not' with 'will use reasonable efforts to avoid,' making compliance subject to effort rather than guaranteed; (2) adding 'knowingly,' which requires proof of intent to violate export controls, making enforcement more difficult; and (3) removing the explicit requirement to obtain necessary licenses/approvals, implying they might only be sought if 'reasonable efforts' dictate.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR imposes strict liability for export control violations, meaning a person can be held liable even without knowledge of the violation in some cases. By adding a 'knowingly' requirement, the modified text contradicts the EAR's principle of strict liability. Furthermore, 15 CFR § 732.3(b) describes the steps you need to take to determine export control requirements, and failing to follow those steps violates export control law. Changing the language to 'reasonable efforts' also contradicts the EAR, which requires affirmative steps to comply with export regulations; reasonable efforts is not strict compliance. This weakens the NDA's protection against export control violations and could expose the disclosing party to liability.

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original strict prohibition on exporting proprietary information in violation of export controls. The original text states the recipient 'agrees that it will not' export, while the changed text states the recipient 'will use reasonable efforts to avoid' exporting. Additionally, the changed text adds 'knowingly,' requiring proof of intent to violate export controls. The law explanation points out that the EAR imposes strict liability, meaning a person can be held liable even without knowledge of the violation. The scraped snippets describe the EAR and steps to determine export control requirements, implying the importance of compliance. The change weakens the obligation, potentially contradicting the strict liability nature of export control regulations.
